title: Discrete Mathematics - Mathematical Logic - UGC NET CSE  June 2013  Part 2  Question

sections.name: UGC NET CSE  June 2013  Part 2  Question

questions.question:
Which of the following shall be a compound proposition involving the propositions p, q and r, that is true when exactly two of the p, q and r are true and is false otherwise?



Mathematical Logic: UGC NET CSE | June 2013 | Part 2 | Question: 39
options: \( (p \vee q \wedge \rceil r) \vee (p \wedge q \wedge r) \wedge (\rceil p \wedge q \vee r) \)
\( (p \wedge q \vee r) \wedge (p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (\rceil q \wedge \rceil q \wedge \rceil r) \)
\( (p \wedge q \wedge \rceil r) \vee (p \wedge \rceil q \wedge r) \vee (\rceil p \wedge q \wedge r) \)
\( (p \vee r \wedge q) \vee (p \wedge q \wedge r) \vee (\rceil p \wedge q \wedge r) \)
questions.correct_answer: \( (p \wedge q \wedge \rceil r) \vee (p \wedge \rceil q \wedge r) \vee (\rceil p \wedge q \wedge r) \)

The tracing model in Prolog describes program execution in terms of certain events. These events are



Mathematical Logic: UGC NET CSE | September 2013 | Part 3 | Question: 67

Which of the following arguments are not valid?

  1. "If Gora gets the job and works hard, then he will be promoted. if Gora gets promotion, then he will be happy. He will not be happy, therefore, either he will not get the job or he will not work hard." 
  2. "Either Puneet is not guilty or Pankaj is telling the truth. Pankaj is not telling the truth, therefore, Puneet is not guilty."
  3. If \( n \) is a real number such that \( n>1 \), then \( n^2 > 1 \). Suppose that \( n^2 > 1 \), then \( n>1 \).


Mathematical Logic: UGC NET CSE | December 2015 | Part 2 | Question: 6

________ predicate calculus allows quantified variables to refer to objects in the domain of discourse and not to predicates or functions.



Mathematical Logic: UGC NET CSE | June 2014 | Part 3 | Question: 32

"If my computations are correct and I pay the electric bill, then I will run out of money. If I don't pay the electric bill, the power will be turned off. Therefore, If I don't run out of money and the power is still on then my computations are incorrect."

Convert this argument into logical notations using the variables \( c, b, r, p \) for propositions of computations, electric bills, out of money and the power respectively. (Where \( \neg \) means NOT).



Mathematical Logic: UGC NET CSE | Junet 2015 | Part 2 | Question: 8
options: \( ext{if } (c \wedge b) ightarrow r ext{ and } eg b ightarrow eg p,  ext{ then } ( eg r \wedge p) ightarrow eg c \)
\( \text{if } (c \vee b) \rightarrow r \text{and } \neg b \rightarrow \neg p, \text{ then } (r \wedge p) \rightarrow c \)
\( ext{if } (c \wedge b) ightarrow r  ext{ and } eg p ightarrow eg b,  ext{ then } ( eg r \vee p) ightarrow eg c \)
\( ext{if } (c \vee b) ightarrow r  ext{ and } eg b ightarrow eg p,  ext{ then } ( eg r \wedge p) ightarrow eg c \)
questions.correct_answer: \( ext{if } (c \wedge b) ightarrow r ext{ and } eg b ightarrow eg p,  ext{ then } ( eg r \wedge p) ightarrow eg c \)

Mathematical Logic: UGC NET CSE | Junet 2015 | Part 3 | Question: 22

Let P(m,n) be the statement "m divides n" where the universe of discourse for both the variable is the set of positive integers. Determine the truth values of each of the following propositions:

  1. \( \forall m \forall n P(m, n) \),
  2. \( \forall n P(1,n) \)
  3. \( \exists m \forall n P(m,n) \)

  3. \( p \wedge (q \vee r) \)

  1. Any tree is \( 2 \)-colorable
